goal: Shift HorrorCorridor away from neon cyber styling and toward a broken, dark, dirty, overgrown city-like maze where grassy terrain, roots, rubble, and modular corridor tiles are procedurally assembled.

use: faint sickly anomaly glow only as an accent
avoid: terminal-grid hologram as the default world style

rootIdeas.hypothesis: The world will feel more physical and less cyber if the floor becomes a terrain domain first: muddy grass, soil, puddles, roots, rubble, and worn path masks.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Convert maze walkable cells into terrain patches with slope, wetness, grass density, mud density, rubble density, and path-wear masks.
acceptance: Terrain descriptors are separate from wall/corridor descriptors.
Walkable maze cells remain collision-safe.
Debug output reports terrain patch counts and density ranges.

children.test: Generate deterministic grass/mud/puddle masks from maze seed and distance to route center.
Keep spawn, anomaly, cube pickups, and core path clear while still dressing edges.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Provide shader descriptors for triplanar or projected terrain layers: soil, moss, grass, water sheen, rough concrete, and packed dirt.
acceptance: Grass and mud are visible in player screenshots.
Terrain reads dirty and natural, not glowing cyber green.
The shader can be tuned by preset without changing generic kit code.

children.test: Blend grass, mud, and broken concrete using world-space noise and cell masks.
Add procedural roughness/wetness variation so puddles and mud catch local light.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Spawn non-blocking grass clumps, weeds, roots, small branches, stones, puddle decals, and trash objects on terrain descriptors.
acceptance: Objects appear on top of terrain, not floating or z-fighting.
Foreground/midground screenshots show at least one terrain object cluster.
Object density stays low enough for route readability.

children.test: Use alpha-clipped instanced grass cards with dirty desaturated color.
Place root/vine strips along wall/floor junctions using adjacency samples.
Add broken bottles, bricks, boards, paper, and small debris as non-blocking props.

risks: Grass cards can look cheap if alpha clipping, lighting, and density are not controlled.
Too much floor clutter can hide route readability and ooze trails.
Green grass can drift back into neon unless palette is desaturated and dirty.

rootIdeas.hypothesis: The maze should be assembled from modular broken-city corridor tiles on top of terrain rather than rendered as one uniform wall/floor grid.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Define reusable tile archetypes: straight, corner, T-junction, cross, dead-end, anomaly approach, collapsed side, narrow choke, and overgrown pocket.
acceptance: Each maze cell or cell cluster resolves to a tile archetype.
Tiles preserve walkable connectivity.
Tile variants are seeded and deterministic.

children.test: Classify each walkable cell by open north/east/south/west neighbors.
Choose visual variants from seed, region, and proximity to objectives.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Generate modular walls from brick, broken concrete, chain-link, boarded gaps, rusted service doors, and collapsed city fragments.
acceptance: Walls no longer read as large flat green slabs.
Brick/concrete modules are visible but not overbright.
Openings, cracks, and damage are decorative first and do not break collision.

children.test: Apply broken brick/concrete triplanar shader to wall modules.
Place cracks, moss, water stains, and dirt at wall/floor seams.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Attach local dressing to tiles: grass pockets, table remains, benches, crates, pipes, vents, cables, boards, signage fragments, and blocked side paths.
acceptance: Each region has recognizable landmarks.
Props do not block required routes in the first pass.
Content density can increase over long-session depth.

children.test: Guarantee at least one visible landmark near spawn, route branches, and anomaly approach.
Increase vegetation, rubble, anomaly residue, and broken-city props deeper in the maze.

risks: Tile modularity can become visually repetitive without variant and dressing rules.
Tile assembly must not collapse the existing prototype maze behavior.
Collision should remain grid-owned until visual tiles are validated.

rootIdeas.hypothesis: Lighting should shift from neon terminal green to practical, dirty environmental light: weak moon/sky leak, sodium spill, warm player light, and rare sickly anomaly accents.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Place dim practical lights from broken fixtures, puddle reflections, open ceiling gaps, and anomaly residue.
acceptance: The scene is readable without feeling cyber.
Green is rare and mainly tied to anomaly/trails.
Light sources visibly shape terrain, grass, and wall modules.

subtests.kitResponsibility: Tune fog around terrain readability: haze should reveal depth, not crush grass/walls into black.
acceptance: Foreground grass and muddy floor remain visible.
Midground route cue remains visible.
Background still falls into horror darkness.

risks: Removing neon green requires alternate readability support from albedo, warm lights, and fog.
Dirty lighting can become brown mush without screenshot gating.

work: Retarget product direction away from neon cyber and toward dirty overgrown broken-city horror.
Add terrain/tile kit contracts before touching renderer behavior.
Add one reference prompt for the overgrown corridor spawn target.
Then implement a small visual spike: terrain shader colors plus alpha-clipped grass cards in solo mode.
validation: Use live-player harness screenshot to check terrain readability, visible grass/rubble, and non-cyber palette.
Record status back into this JSON module.
